exemplar.title: The degree of rotation of light by sugar solution through two polarisers

detailedExemplarData.title: The degree of rotation of light by sugar solution through two polarisers

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Data tables are clearly laid out with units, uncertainties and multiple trials.
Processed results (averages, Malus-law transformations, specific rotation calculations) are presented graphically with error bars and best-fit equations.
The calculation steps are transparent and the high RÂ² value is clearly communicated, reinforcing the validity of the linear model.
weaknesses: A path-length inconsistency between stated and used values (9.50 cm vs 0.45 m) introduces potential confusion.
Significant-figure inconsistencies and a few typographical errors reduce overall precision.
Uncertainties in polariser alignment (Â±5Â°) and concentration preparation are not fully propagated through to the final results.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The studentâs communication of data recording and processing is both clear and preciseâunits, uncertainties and graphical presentations are handled expertly. Although there are minor lapses in consistency and propagation of every uncertainty, the overall data-analysis protocol is carried out appropriately and accurately, meriting the top-band score of 6.

strengths: The research question is framed within a highly specific and appropriate context (optical rotation in food and pharmaceutical quality control).
Extensive theoretical grounding on circular polarisation, chirality, Malusâs law and specific rotation clarifies purpose and scope.
The methodological description (screenâbeakerâpolariserâphone sensor arrangement, concentration range, dilution sequence, multiple trials) is sufficiently detailed for reproduction.
weaknesses: The procedure for preparing dilutions lacks explicit detail on the measurement tools and associated error margins.
The exact jig or alignment tool for achieving the 90Â° polariser setting is not specified.
The calibration method for the mobile lux sensor app and criteria used in pre-trial refinements are not fully described.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although minor ambiguities remain in jig alignment and dilution preparation, the studentâs embedding of the research question in a precise real-world context, combined with thorough theoretical justification and largely reproducible methodology, meets the requirements of the top band (5â6). The omissions are relatively small and do not undermine overall reproducibility, justifying a full score of 6.

strengths: The conclusion is directly justified by the processed data and high RÂ² value, affirming the stated linear relationship.
A literature comparison yields a measured specific rotation versus accepted values and a calculated percent error.
The student acknowledges and briefly discusses an alternative polynomial fit, demonstrating analytical breadth.
weaknesses: The unexplained non-zero y-intercept is noted but not rationalized, leaving part of the rationale incomplete.
The discussion of discrepancies (e.g., temperature or wavelength dispersion effects) is too brief to deepen scientific context.
Minor numerical slips slightly weaken the sense of a fully polished justification.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is well supported and clearly linked to analysis, with relevant scientific context provided through literature comparison. Minor unexplained elements (y-intercept, limited discussion of dispersion) prevent a perfect top-band performance but nonetheless reflect a strong, consistent justification, warranting 5 out of 6.

strengths: Specific methodological weaknesses (lux-meter resolution, polariser misalignment, glass-induced depolarisation) are identified with qualitative impact on accuracy and precision.
Realistic improvements (angle-locking mount, aerogel container, more precise probe, additional trials) are relevant and feasible.
Creative linkage to literature (use of aerogel) shows depth of engagement with error mitigation.
weaknesses: There is no quantitative estimation of how each suggested improvement would reduce uncertainty.
The relative impact of listed weaknesses is not ranked or compared, limiting prioritization of further work.
Suggested enhancements lack explanations of expected magnitude of improvement.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student describes specific limitations and proposes realistic improvements but stops short of explaining how much each change would improve accuracy or precision. This performance aligns with the middle band (3â4) for Evaluation, meriting a score of 4.

strandSelections.reasoning: The student formulates a focused research question and immediately embeds it in the specific context of optical rotation, outlining real-world uses in food and pharmaceutical quality control and explaining why a laptop LCD/polariser arrangement is appropriate.  Background theory on circular polarisation, chirality, Malusâs law and specific rotation makes the purpose and scope of the investigation very clear.  Because the context is both specific and appropriate, the work aligns with the top-band descriptor, though a brief mention of competing methods (e.g. commercial polarimeters) would have further strengthened the contextual framing.

Instrument uncertainties are quoted, propagated to percentage uncertainty in intensity and angle, and displayed as error bars.  Nevertheless, concentration uncertainty is ignored, the Â±5Â° alignment error is not propagated, and error bars on the graph appear uniform despite differing percentage values.  These omissions indicate partial, not fully appropriate, treatment of uncertainties, placing the work in the middle band.

Control of temperature, path length, ambient light and screen intensity is stated, and pre-trial work to refine the set-up is described, showing clear awareness of factors affecting data quality.  However, explanations of *why* each control matters are brief; uncertainty in the 90Â° alignment and purity of commercial sucrose are acknowledged but not quantitatively assessed.  These elements amount to a description of considerations relevant to collecting *sufficient* data, meeting the 3-4 descriptor, yet they stop short of the fuller justification required for the top band.

Specific weaknessesâlux-meter resolution, misalignment of polarisers, glass-induced depolarisationâare identified, and their likely effects (systematic upward shift, increased scatter) are noted.  The explanations, however, remain qualitative and do not quantify the magnitude of each error or rank their relative impact, placing the discussion at the descriptive level corresponding to 3-4 marks.

The method describes the screenâbeakerâpolariserâphone-sensor arrangement, the concentration range, the dilution sequence and the procedure for recording four trials at each concentration.  A knowledgeable reader could reproduce the investigation, but there are ambiguities: the exact orientation tool for the 90Â° polariser setting, the way dilutions were quantified, and the calibration of the phone lux app are not specified.  Hence the level of detail satisfies reproducibility "with few ambiguities", matching the 3-4 band, but does not merit the highest band where no substantive omissions are present.

Averages, Malus-law transformations and specific-rotation calculations are carried out logically and lead to a linear fit with RÂ² quoted.  Minor errors (path-length taken as 0.45 m for a 9.5 cm beaker, inconsistent significant figures, mis-typed symbols) show lapses in accuracy.  Processing is therefore adequate but contains notable inconsistencies, satisfying the 3-4 descriptor.

Improvement suggestionsâuse of aerogel container, more precise intensity probe, angle-locking mount, additional trialsâare relevant and feasible.  Yet the student seldom explains *how much* each change would reduce uncertainty or address specific errors, so the proposals are described rather than fully explained, matching the middle-band descriptor.

Data tables include units, uncertainties and multiple trials; processed values are presented graphically with error bars and best-fit equations.  Some typographical issues (mis-spelled headers, uneven significant figures) reduce precision, but overall the presentation is clear enough for the reader to follow calculations.  Because communication is clear *or* precise but not consistently both, the 3-4 band is the most appropriate.

The measured specific rotation is compared with a literature value, a percentage error is calculated, and supporting literature (Harvie et al.) is cited to justify the expected linearity.  This represents a relevant comparison, but the discussion is brief and does not explore temperature dependence or wavelength dispersion that would deepen the justification.  Hence the strand aligns with the 3-4 band.

The student states a linear relationship supported by the high RÂ² value, discusses an alternative polynomial fit, and links the conclusion explicitly to the processed data.  While the relationship is broadly consistent with the analysis, minor numerical slips and the unexplained y-intercept prevent the conclusion from being *fully* consistent and justified, situating it near the top of the 3-4 band.

annotations.comment: The description of how dilutions were prepared lacks detail on measurement technique and error margins, making reproduction of concentration steps ambiguous.
The research question is placed within a specific and appropriate context, linking optical rotation theory to practical applications and clarifying the investigationâs purpose.
Propagate the Â±5Â° polariser alignment uncertainty through to the calculated angle Ï to present a more complete uncertainty analysis.
The description of the best-fit line and high RÂ² value communicates the data processing outcome clearly, helping to validate the linear model.
The conclusion notes an alternate polynomial fit but does not justify why it was excluded, leaving the rationale incomplete.
The statement that uncertainties âare relatively lowâ requires context or benchmarks to substantiate why 5â13% is acceptable in this investigation.
The suggested improvements (e.g., angle-locking mount) lack quantitative predictions of how much precision or accuracy would improve.
Expand the discussion to include how temperature variation or wavelength dispersion might affect specific rotation, deepening the scientific context.
The qualitative data table is rich in description but is not directly linked to quantitative trends; consider integrating observations with numerical results.
Specify calibration details for the light sensor app (model, calibration checks) to strengthen credibility of intensity measurements.
Specify the tool or jig used to set the polariser at exactly 90Â°, and describe how its alignment accuracy was verified.
Background explanation of polarisation and sugarâs interaction is thorough and uses credible sources to support theoretical grounding.
How do the theoretical concepts of circular polarisation directly inform the choice of measurement angles and data analysis steps?
Consider adding labels or annotations to the EM wave diagrams to clarify axes and helicity directions for the reader.
The derivation and presentation of Malusâs law adaptation are clear and logically connected to the experimental use of intensity-to-angle conversion.
The pre-trial narrative omits the specific criteria used to assess and refine each setup, limiting insight into how precision improvements were judged.
Detail the calibration procedure and model of the mobile lux sensor app to demonstrate its reliability and any pre-experiment checks performed.
Including uncertainty values for all control variables reflects a strong awareness of measurement quality and potential error sources.
The listed path length of 9.50 cm contradicts the 0.45 m used in calculations, introducing potential confusion in data processing.
Why was the initial screen intensity set at 499 luxâwas this value based on preliminary tests, and how was it verified to be stable throughout trials?
The raw data table presents four trials with uncertainties, which supports transparent assessment of measurement variability.
Consider including the uncertainty in concentration preparation in the tables, propagating solute mass and volume errors into final % uncertainties.
The step-by-step calculation of average intensity using Equation 2 is clear, aiding reader comprehension of data processing.
The processed data table uses inconsistent decimal places (e.g., angles reported to three decimals vs. two), reducing the precision of presented results.
Identifying the use of an aerogel container to mitigate glass-induced systematic error shows creative linking of literature to experimental improvement.

rubricData.name: Physics IA
rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â
